planting.note: Plant fresh (unsoaked) nuts in fall in moist, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, deep, well-drained soil in partial shade; unlike most trees, yellow buckeye performs best in partial shade, especially afternoon shade in zone 7–8. The fresh nuts lose viability rapidly — plant within weeks of collection.

directions: Plant in moist, fertile, deep, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade — yellow buckeye is unusual among trees in that it actually performs better in partial shade than full sun, making it one of the best large native trees for the shaded garden. Native to rich mountain forests of the southern Appalachians, it produces beautiful upright clusters of yellow flowers in spring that attract hummingbirds and long-tongued native bees, and large smooth seed pods containing 1–3 mahogany-brown nuts with pale patches — striking ornamental fall fruit. The palmate compound leaves of 5 leaflets are among the first to emerge in spring and first to drop in fall (often by August in drought). It tolerates understory conditions and is frequently found growing as a subcanopy tree beneath tulip poplar and basswood. The nuts are toxic to humans but consumed by squirrels. Often considered the most beautiful of the North American buckeyes.

planting.note: Plant fresh acorns in fall or container trees in early spring in well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to neutral, deep soil in full sun; Shumard oak establishes faster than most oaks and tolerates brief flooding, making it an excellent street and yard tree in the South.

directions: Plant fresh acorns in fall or container trees in early spring in well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to neutral, deep soil in full sun; Shumard oak is the premier large native oak of the Gulf Coast and Deep South, growing faster than most oaks (2–3 feet per year when young) while developing into a large, long-lived specimen tree with spectacular deep red-to-scarlet fall color — one of the best oaks for fall color in zone 7–9 where many trees fail to color reliably. It tolerates brief flooding and clay soils better than many oaks, making it an excellent choice for urban and suburban landscapes in the South. The deeply lobed leaves with pointed tips are similar to pin oak but without the drooping lower branches. The acorns provide important fall food for turkeys, deer, and numerous bird species. An outstanding Gulf Coast street tree, shade tree, and wildlife tree.

planting.note: Plant bare-root or container shrubs in late winter or early spring in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial shade; gooseberries may be illegal to plant in some US states where they are regulated as alternate hosts of white pine blister rust — check local regulations before planting.

directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial shade to full sun — American gooseberry is the native gooseberry of the northeastern US and Canada, one of the most productive edible native shrubs for cool-climate food gardens. The tart-sweet berries (ripening from green to reddish-purple in midsummer) are excellent for pies, jams, preserves, and fresh eating when fully ripe. The shrub tolerates partial shade better than most fruiting shrubs and produces reliably in conditions too shaded for currants and jostaberries. The stems bear short, sharp spines at the nodes; wear gloves when harvesting. American gooseberry is more resistant to powdery mildew than European gooseberry (R. uva-crispa), making it easier to grow in humid climates. Best in zones 3–7; does not perform well in hot, humid summers of zone 8 and south. Check state regulations on Ribes planting before ordering.

planting.note: Direct sow seed in early spring in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial shade; plants spread steadily by rhizome and can naturalize in appropriate moist woodland settings. Do not confuse with purple loosestrife (Lythrum salicaria), an invasive wetland plant — this is a completely different, well-behaved native species.

directions: Direct sow in early spring or plant divisions in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial shade; whorled loosestrife is a charming native woodland wildflower of eastern North America, recognizable by its distinctive architecture — the lance-shaped leaves are arranged in precise whorls of 4 around the stem, and small five-petaled yellow flowers with a red center arise from the axil of each leaf in the whorl, creating a very regular, geometric flowering pattern in early summer. It is in no way related to the invasive purple loosestrife (Lythrum) — this is a well-behaved native Lysimachia that spreads slowly by rhizome in appropriate moist, acidic woodland conditions. It provides early-summer pollen and floral oils for specialist native bees in the family Macropis. A lovely, understated wildflower for the naturalized woodland garden.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; wild mint spreads vigorously by rhizome and should be contained in a pot or planting bed edged with a barrier, or allowed to naturalize in a wet, shaded area where spreading is welcome.

directions: Plant divisions in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; wild mint is North America's only native mint species and has the widest natural range of any mint in the world — native from the Atlantic Coast to Asia across the boreal zone. It grows naturally at stream banks, pond margins, moist meadows, and woodland edges, forming spreading mats of intensely aromatic leaves with a clean mint-spearmint fragrance used exactly like cultivated spearmint for teas, cooking, and flavoring. The small lavender-purple flower whorls in summer attract numerous native bees including specialized pollen bees in the genus Cleptes. Unlike garden mints, it is fully native and supports native insects while providing the same culinary utility. It tolerates partial shade and moist soil conditions that are inhospitable to most culinary herbs. Contain with underground barriers or allow to naturalize in appropriate wet areas.
